title: 1RM Back Squat Calculator 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
description: Free 1RM back squat calculator using Epley formula. Quickly estimate your max lift for strength training.
intro: Strength training is built on physiological principles—adaptation, supercompensation, and specificity. Intensity zones, weekly training volume, and recovery are the foundations of performance. This 1RM back squat calculator uses the Epley formula to quickly estimate your one-rep max. Values are updated for 2026 and reviewed regularly to keep you current.
keyTakeaway: Formula: **1RM = weight × (1 + reps/30)**. Direct calculation using your inputs. Fast 1RM back squat estimation via Epley.

explanation:
**Strength training** combines science (physiology, biomechanics) and practice. Intensity zones, progressive overload, adequate rest, and proper nutrition form the complete training system.

## How to Calculate

This calculator applies the Epley formula to your inputs, checking for realistic ranges and alerting you if values seem unusual or outside typical training parameters.

## Important Notes

This calculation is a reference estimate only. For critical decisions (athletic performance, medical concerns), consult a qualified strength coach or sports medicine professional. Values are current as of 2026 and reviewed regularly.

faq.a: The Epley formula is 1RM = weight × (1 + reps/30). It estimates your one-rep max from submaximal lifts. More reps completed = less accuracy in the estimate.
The Epley formula is most accurate for 1-10 reps. Beyond 10 reps, estimates tend to underestimate your true one-rep max.

faq.q: Should I test my actual 1RM or use this calculator?
faq.a: A true 1RM test gives the exact number but carries injury risk. Use this calculator to estimate safely first, then test in person with proper form and spotters.

faq.q: How many reps should I use for accurate estimation?
faq.a: The Epley formula works best with 2-10 reps at a submaximal weight. Stay within this range for the most accurate estimates.

faq.q: How do I improve my back squat strength?
faq.a: Use linear progression with moderate volume and adequate rest (48+ hours) between sessions. Progressive overload and proper form are critical.

faq.q: What's the proper warmup before testing 1RM?
faq.a: Use an ascending pyramid: light weight for 5-10 reps, then progressively heavier sets with fewer reps and 2-3 minute rest between heavy sets.

faq.q: Is this calculator free to use?
faq.a: Yes, all our calculators are completely free with no registration or account required.

faq.q: Do you collect or store my data?
faq.a: No. All calculations run entirely in your browser. We don't send or store any personal data to our servers.

sources.name: NIST — National Institute of Standards and Technology

dataUpdate.notes: Fixed formula, does not require external data updates
